problem_summary: 2023 MCM Problem B (discrete/policy modeling): build a management/sustainability model for the Maasai Mara National Preserve in Kenya, balancing environment, economy (tourism), and the local Maasai population, then recommend optimal government policies. This team (control number 2318300, Outstanding award) framed the entire preserve as one coupled dynamical system of 6 interdependent processes — prey, predators, tourism, environmental degradation, poaching, and retaliatory killings — and turned "policy design" into a constrained optimization over the subset of model parameters the Kenyan government can directly fund. Their headline deliverable is a KSh379M annual budget (a 46% increase) allocated across anti-poaching, environment, the Maasai population, and the local economy, projected to yield +24% prey, +78% predators, -19% degradation, -97% retaliatory killings, -50% poaching, +7.5% tourism at the new equilibrium.
models_used: Modified Lotka-Volterra predator-prey system (prey made logistic with carrying capacity k - eta*Degradation; predator growth proportional to prey)
6-equation system of coupled first-order ODEs (Prey, Predator, Tourism, Degradation, Poaching, Retaliatory Killings) solved numerically
Gordon-Schaefer bioeconomic harvesting model (from fisheries) to represent poaching as a harvest term and as a revenue-minus-cost effort equation
'Minimal model' of tourism sustainability (Casagrandi-Rinaldi style) for the Tourism-Degradation coupling, tuned to produce near-sinusoidal seasonal cycles
Equilibrium / nullcline analysis: solved each ODE = 0 to get a closed-form non-trivial equilibrium point parameterized by tau = (E_r+I_0-G)/(P_t-I_t)
Jacobian linearization + eigenvalue analysis at the equilibrium to prove local asymptotic stability (all eigenvalues have negative real part; spiral-sink behavior)
Utility/social-benefit function U(X) = Y(X)·w - Cost(X) with quadratic cost c(X·X) modeling diminishing returns, over 8 government-controllable parameters
L-BFGS-B bound-constrained quasi-Newton optimization to maximize utility and produce the optimal parameter (budget) allocation
One-At-a-Time (OAT) sensitivity analysis via re-computing Jacobian eigenvalues across each parameter's range
key_techniques: Reframing an open-ended policy question as parameter optimization: identify which of the 26 parameters the government can actually fund (they chose 8: A, I_0, P_t, I_t, lambda_0, lambda, B, Omega), optimize only those.

Closed-form equilibrium via a single pivot variable tau (the tourism nullcline), then expressing every other nullcline as a function of tau — this collapses a 6-D fixed-point problem into algebra and makes the Jacobian tractable.
Using the analytic equilibrium as the input to the utility function, so 'long-term outcome' = equilibrium coordinates Y, avoiding expensive transient simulation inside the optimizer.

Engineered weight vector w to encode value judgments transparently: w0=1e6 (prey), w2=1e6 (tourists), w1=1e3 (predators), w3=0 (poaching neutral), w4=-1e3 (retaliatory killings penalized as a proxy for harm to Maasai livestock).
Calibrating cost weight c=5e5 against the actual FY2019-20 preserve budget so the optimizer's output reads in real Kenyan Shillings.

Anchoring most of the 26 parameters to literature/data (Lotka-Volterra wildebeest-lion control papers, poacher-population dynamics, Serengeti carrying capacity) and explicitly labeling each parameter as literature / data / subjectively-estimated.
Time-unit damper (2/5 on gamma) to map the model's ~150-unit numerical cycle onto a 1-day timestep so results read in calendar time.

Two-layer policy output: objective optimizer ranking, then a 'subjective policy' layer translating each high-ranked parameter into 2-3 concrete real-world interventions (CCTV from lodges, ranger airstrips, Maasai Stoves & Solar, turning Narok Town into a resort city).
why_it_won: "Judges reward a model that is simultaneously principled and decision-useful, and this paper nails both. (1) It grounds every one of its 6 equations in a named, peer-reviewed model (Lotka-Volterra, Gordon-Schaefer, the tourism minimal model) rather than inventing ad hoc dynamics, giving the system instant credibility. (2) It does real applied-math work most teams skip: it derives a closed-form equilibrium, computes the Jacobian symbolically, and proves asymptotic stability via eigenvalues — turning 'we ran a simulation' into 'we understand the system's long-run behavior analytically,' which is exactly what justifies steady-state policy inference. (3) The optimization layer is the winning move: it converts the vague 'recommend policies' prompt into a clean, bounded optimization (L-BFGS-B over 8 fundable parameters) whose output is a ranked, costed budget in real currency. (4) It closes the loop back to the human problem — every optimized parameter is mapped to concrete, locally-grounded interventions and to the Maasai stakeholders — and it ends with a standalone, jargon-free committee report. (5) It is unusually honest: a 6-strength / 6-weakness section plus generalization discussion signals mathematical maturity. The combination of literature-anchored dynamics + analytic stability proof + currency-denominated optimization + stakeholder framing is what separates an Outstanding paper from a Meritorious one."
section_highlights: "Strong structural choices worth copying: (a) A 1-page visual 'overview of our dynamic model' (Figure 1 causal graph with green=positive / red=negative arrows among the 6 processes) placed BEFORE the math, so a reader grasps the whole system in 30 seconds. (b) A rigorously enumerated, ID-tagged assumptions section (M-1/M-2 for the preserve, F-1..F-6 fauna, T-1..T-4 tourism, E-1..E-4 environment, P-1..P-5 poaching, RK-1..RK-3) — each assumption is later traceable to a specific equation term. (c) Equation construction is narrated term-by-term: they show the bare Lotka-Volterra core, then explicitly justify every added term (poaching harvest, migration m0 - m*Degradation, tourist-distraction -S*Tourism, boma offset -B*Tourism). (d) Parameter provenance is split into three honest buckets (from literature / from data / calculated-subjective) with citations, plus full parameter Tables 1-3. (e) Results are reported as concrete percentage deltas at equilibrium and as a real KSh budget table. (f) A dedicated Section 11.1 on generalizing to other preserves (cell-splitting for larger parks, swapping the boma/revenge-killing terms, handling parks without poaching or with invasive species). (g) The closing Section 15 is a clean client-facing committee report with a budget-by-sector table and prioritized policies, written without equations — strong audience awareness."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Tourism and Degradation form a closed 2-equation subsystem driven only by each other — fauna health does NOT feed back into tourism or degradation. This is unrealistic (Maasai Mara tourism is ecotourism; collapsing fauna would cut tourism) and the team admits it as weakness #2.

Hard-coded assumption that pollution per tourist P_t always exceeds investment per tourist I_t (so tourism is always net-negative on environment), which by construction forbids analyzing any 'green tourism pays for itself' policy.

Single global cost weight c applied to all 8 parameters means 'cost = parameter value * c', so the reported per-parameter KSh costs are essentially the parameter magnitudes rescaled — the team flags this as a flaw; some parameters (e.g. Omega, opportunity cost of poaching) are far harder/costlier to move than the linear cost implies.
Climate change modeled as a fixed constant G rather than a time-increasing or stochastic process, so no climate-resilience policy can be evaluated.
Maasai population growth (400k -> 1.1M) is acknowledged but not modeled, despite directly driving retaliatory killings and degradation.
All fauna collapsed into two buckets (predator/prey) with no biodiversity/species structure; large-predator-hunts-large-prey nuance lost.

Weight vector w (1e6 / 1e3 / 0 / -1e3) is chosen subjectively and drives the entire optimizer output — the dramatic results (+78% predators, -97% retaliatory killings) are conditional on these value judgments, not robustly derived.

Several figures in the rendered markdown are garbled (Figure 5's I_0 axis shows nonsense values like ~67477777777777777), and the appendix code is only an external link — reproducibility from the document alone is limited.
Many parameters are 'subjective estimations informed by literature' with no uncertainty quantification beyond OAT; OAT misses interaction effects between parameters.
reusable_patterns: 'Causal graph first, equations second': open with a signed-arrow influence diagram of all state variables so the grader sees the system before the calculus.
ID-tagged assumptions (M-1, F-3, P-2, ...) that are later cited by name when justifying each equation term — makes the model auditable and shows discipline.

Build coupled-ODE systems by starting from a canonical named model (Lotka-Volterra / Gordon-Schaefer / tourism minimal model) and adding clearly-justified perturbation terms, rather than inventing dynamics from scratch — cite the canonical model explicitly.

Solve for a closed-form equilibrium by finding one 'pivot' nullcline (here tau from the simplest equation) and expressing all other equilibrium coordinates as functions of it; then linearize via the Jacobian and read off stability from eigenvalue signs to justify steady-state analysis.

Turn 'recommend a policy' into 'maximize a utility/social-benefit function over only the decision-maker-controllable parameters,' using a bound-constrained optimizer (L-BFGS-B) so outputs respect real limits.
Make utility = (weighted equilibrium outcomes) - (cost), calibrate the cost weight against the real-world budget so optimizer output is denominated in actual currency the client understands.
Encode stakeholder priorities explicitly and transparently in a weight vector, then state them in prose so graders can see the value judgments rather than hunting for hidden ones.
Two-layer results: quantitative optimizer ranking + a 'subjective policy' translation of each parameter into concrete, locally-named real-world actions.
OAT sensitivity by re-running Jacobian eigenvalues across each parameter range, reporting which parameters can flip stability (here I_t<P_t and m<53.77 are the stability boundaries).
Close with (a) an explicit 'how to generalize this model elsewhere' section and (b) a standalone, equation-free client memo with a budget-by-sector table — strong for the MCM communication score.
